topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email sat glowing on my screen, a stark white beacon in the dim glow of my desk lamp. Dr. Albright’s departure was confirmed. My hands, resting on the keyboard, began to tremble almost imperceptibly. I reread the final paragraph, the polite farewells feeling like sharp little needles against my chest. Every time I looked up, my gaze snagged on the empty chair across the mahogany table in the faculty lounge, a phantom presence I desperately wished would reappear. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor, and walked to the window, staring out at the sprawling campus as if the answer to my impending academic void might be visible in the distant horizon. My breath hitched, shallow and quick.

[story 2]

I kept smoothing the edges of my already perfectly ironed shirt, a repetitive motion that served only to distract from the churning in my stomach. When the news finally landed, it felt less like information and more like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. My fingers tightened around my lukewarm coffee mug until the ceramic threatened to crack. I tried to focus on the bibliography I was supposed to be finalizing, but the words swam. A sudden clamminess spread across my palms. I chewed on my lower lip, the taste of metal sharp in my mouth. I kept glancing at the clock; the seconds seemed to stretch into agonizing minutes. I needed to formulate a response, something steady, but all that came out was a strangled sound caught in my throat.

[story 3]

The silence in my small apartment amplified everything. I paced the worn rug, the rhythm of my footsteps uneven and fast. The notification had been innocuous, a simple administrative update, but the implication—Dr. Albright leaving—sent a shockwave through my system. I stopped pacing abruptly, leaning against the doorframe, trying to anchor myself. My heart was hammering against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat demanding attention. I picked up my phone, intending to call someone, but my thumb hovered over the dial pad. Instead, I just stared at the ceiling, cataloging the shadows dancing on the plaster, wishing with a sudden, intense yearning that the whole situation could just dissolve into nothingness.
